target.name: Mainspring Energy
target.founder: Shannon Miller, Adam Simpson, Matt Svrcek

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series E → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.4x) to $400M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 21 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
Mainspring Energy is a Series E climate and energy company that has raised $400M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws on a frontier-tech comparables universe of 9 private peers (including TAE Technologies, Redwood Materials, Helion, and Commonwealth Fusion Systems) and 1 public comparable (Linnea Oil Company, LNZA), though meaningful peer-median triangulation was constrained by the absence of three or more same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables with sufficient overlap in valuation tier.

Stage-progression methodology, the primary applicable approach, uses the $400M total raised as a valuation proxy and applies a 1.4x step-up multiplier typical of Series E to later-stage transitions, yielding an implied valuation of $560M. This method reflects the capital intensity and extended development timelines characteristic of frontier energy technologies, where fundraising rounds often serve as a reliable intermediate signal of investor conviction and embedded valuation.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $476M (low) to $644M (median to high), with a point estimate of $560M. The range reflects reasonable sensitivity around stage-progression inputs and the typical bounds of Series E step-up multiples in capital-intensive sectors. Key uncertainties include the absence of direct same-stage peer metrics, which limits external validation, and the company's technology maturity—factors that would benefit from qualitative review of development stage, customer traction, and IP strength to refine the valuation band.
